topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The calendar page felt heavy in Maya’s hand, the date circled aggressively in red ink. Across the table, Liam was already sketching out decorations for his themed gathering, a vibrant explosion of neon and glitter. Maya traced the outline of her own, much more subdued, plans. A cold knot tightened in her stomach as she watched him, radiating pure, unadulterated joy. She fiddled with the edge of her napkin, the crisp paper a stark contrast to the churning feeling inside her. *Why am I doing this?* The thought echoed hollowly. When Liam finally looked up, his smile blinding, Maya managed a tight, practiced laugh that sounded brittle even to her own ears. "So, the backyard bash is going to be epic," she managed, forcing a bright tone that didn't reach her eyes. She quickly shifted her gaze to the window, focusing intently on a passing car, desperate to disappear into the glass.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the way Liam’s excitement had been infectious, and how my own internal landscape felt like a desolate, grey room. Planning two separate celebrations for my birthday on the very same afternoon felt like a monumental failure. My hands were slick with sweat as I meticulously organized the guest list for my quieter, more intimate gathering. Every checkmark felt like a small, painful admission of inadequacy. When Liam suggested we coordinate, his enthusiasm was genuine, but it only amplified the tightness in my chest. "We could make it a double event!" he proposed, leaning forward, his voice warm and inviting. I looked down at my shoes, my knuckles white where I gripped the strap of my bag. "No, no, I think I'll just keep things simple," I replied, the words coming out clipped and too fast. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to retreat, to simply vanish from the shared space.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun beat down, making the pavement shimmer. I stood by the mailbox, staring at the invitation I had just finished drafting—a simple gathering for a handful of close friends. The sheer audacity of it, planning a separate event when Liam was planning something so grand, made my throat constrict. Every time I imagined him laughing with his crowd, a deep, unpleasant hollowness settled in my chest. I started pacing the small front yard, my steps quick and uneven. I caught sight of Liam across the street, already setting up banners. A wave of self-reproach washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I pulled my shoulders inward, hunching slightly, trying to make myself smaller, less visible, hoping the shadows would swallow me whole.
